--- render_with_liquid: false --- Federated Data Science Demo — Rhino Federated Computing
Rhino Federated Computing Platform
Real-World Evidence · Oncology
Two hospitals that can't share a single patient record — building one cancer model together.
A US oncology network and an EU clinical registry collaborate to predict which patients will respond to a targeted therapy. No raw patient record ever leaves either institution. Here is how — and the proof it works.

Can two hospitals help each other without sharing data?

A targeted cancer therapy works wonderfully for some patients and not at all for others. A model that could tell the two apart — before treatment starts — would spare patients months on a drug that won't help them. The catch: no single hospital has treated enough patients to build that model reliably, and the law won't let them combine their records to try.

This notebook tells the story of how they do it anyway — across a US oncology network and an EU clinical registry, 3,588 patients in total, without a single record ever leaving either institution. It unfolds in four phases.

1 · CONNECT
Join one governed workspace; point at each hospital's data where it already lives.
2 · EXPLORE
Get to know both patient groups — without moving any data.
3 · TRAIN & PROVE
Train one shared model — and prove it beats going it alone.
4 · DEPLOY
Put the model to work inside each hospital, on new patients.

The impossible problem — and the way around it

Better medical AI needs more patients. The strongest model here would learn from both the US network and the EU registry at once — more patients, and a wider range of them, than either site has alone. But these are real patient records, governed by HIPAA in the US, GDPR in the EU, and each institution's own rules. The data legally cannot be pooled into one place. And the more patients you want, the more institutions you need — which makes pooling the data even less possible.

The way around it is to flip the usual approach. Instead of moving the data to the computation, you move the computation to the data. This is called federated computing (a way to run the math inside each hospital, so raw records never travel). A central coordinator sends the task out to each hospital; each hospital runs it on its own patients, behind its own firewall; and only the result — a count, an average, a model update — comes back. The patient records stay exactly where they are.

The core loop: the task travels out to each hospital's data, the data never travels back.

The guarantee: Every calculation runs inside the hospital that owns the data. Only anonymous summaries — counts, averages, model updates — ever cross the network. No individual patient record, lab value, or treatment outcome leaves the institution that holds it.

1
Phase 1 of 4
Connect
A data scientist connects to two hospital databases — one US, one EU — through a single governed workspace.

Before any analysis, both hospitals agree to work together inside one shared, governed workspace. That workspace sets the ground rules up front: who takes part, who is allowed to run what, and privacy limits set in advance — so nothing happens that the data owners did not approve.

Crucially, the hospitals do not upload anything. Rhino's connectors (plug-ins that read each hospital's existing data systems — data warehouses like Snowflake or Databricks, or cloud storage — without copying the data) simply point at the records where they already live. The data stays inside each hospital's walls; the platform just gets a secure way to reach it.

The guarantee: The data never moves — the platform only gets a secure way to reach each hospital's records where they already sit.

Authenticate below, and the platform confirms the workspace, its governance rules, and the two hospitals taking part.

import rhino_health as rh
from rhino_sdk_utils import *
from op_credentials import get_credentials

PROJECT_NAME = "Oncology Real-World Evidence Study"  # ← update
SITE_US_DATASET = "Dataset A.1"  # ← update
SITE_EU_DATASET = "Dataset B"  # ← update
SITE_US_NAME = "Hospital 1"  # ← update (display name for US hospital)
SITE_EU_NAME = "Hospital 2"  # ← update (display name for EU hospital)
TEST_DATASET = "Dataset B"  # ← update
CODE_OBJECT_NAME = "XGBoost 2.7"  # ← update

USERNAME, PASSWORD = get_credentials("Solutions Env", "Project Lead [Solutions Env]")
session = rh.login(username=USERNAME, rhino_api_url=rh.ApiEnvironment.SOLUTIONS_GCP_URL, password=PASSWORD)

projects = session.project.get_projects()
project = next((p for p in projects if p.name == PROJECT_NAME), None)

print_auth_summary(session, project, username=USERNAME)
Successfully Authenticated to Rhino Platform
User: sam.smith@projectlead.org

Working on Project 'Oncology Real-World Evidence Study'
Project UID: e41363b4-8efb-4df4-8bd3-a27cf6506b18

Point at the data where it already lives

There is no migration project and no central copy. Each hospital's dataset stays exactly where it is — in its existing data systems — a warehouse like Snowflake or Databricks, or cloud storage, on whichever cloud they use — and the connectors hand the platform a secure reference it can call on demand.

With those references in hand, Rhino's Python toolkit (the small library a data scientist writes in to drive the whole workflow) can send work to each hospital by name, and every computation runs at the hospital that owns the data.

hospital_1_data = session.dataset.get_dataset_by_name(SITE_US_DATASET, project_uid=str(project.uid))
hospital_2_data = session.dataset.get_dataset_by_name(SITE_EU_DATASET, project_uid=str(project.uid))

all_uids = [str(hospital_1_data.uid), str(hospital_2_data.uid)]
print_registered_datasets(session, [(hospital_1_data, "US"), (hospital_2_data, "EU")])
Datasets Registered By Collaborators:

Site 1 (Location: US)
Dataset UID: 0d3a9227-e561-46e6-b5c6-956afb964b8d

Site 2 (Location: EU)
Dataset UID: dcda22de-7377-47e6-93f8-03893f9bf94d
2
Phase 2 of 4
Getting to know the patients

Can we learn from data we are not allowed to see?

Before training anything, we need to understand who is in each hospital's cohort — how many patients, how sick they are, how often they respond to treatment. The catch: we are not permitted to look at a single patient record. So instead of pulling the data to us, we send the questions to the data.

We ask each hospital its questions through an AI-assistant interface (a way to query each hospital's data in plain language). Each hospital answers with aggregate statistics (group-level summaries like counts, averages, and rates — never individual records). To make sure a number means the same thing on both sides of the Atlantic, a harmonization step (tooling that lines up the two datasets so the same column means the same thing at both sites) reconciles how each hospital labels its data first.

The guarantee: Only group-level summaries come back — never a single patient record.

summary = fetch_cohort_summary(session, hospital_1_data, hospital_2_data, all_uids)
Rhino Server contacting Site 1…
✓  Site 1 — 1,403 patients | Mean Age: 24.8 yrs | Response Rate: 33.0%

Rhino Server contacting Site 2…
✓  Site 2 — 2,184 patients | Mean Age: 30.6 yrs | Response Rate: 12.3%

✓  Cohort summary ready — 3,588 patients across both hospitals | Federated Response Rate: 20.4%

Are these two hospitals even treating the same kind of patient?

We compare the clinical mix at each site — how many other chronic conditions patients carry, and how long ago they were diagnosed. If the two groups differ, that is good news: a model that sees both learns patterns neither hospital could see alone.

k-anonymization: The platform can enforce a minimum count threshold on any aggregate query — buckets with fewer than k patients are suppressed rather than returned, preventing re-identification from small group sizes. Set minimum_count=k in the metric configuration to enable it.

age_a, age_b = fetch_age_bands(session, hospital_1_data, hospital_2_data)
covar_a, covar_b = fetch_covariate_distributions(session, hospital_1_data, hospital_2_data)
plot_demographics(age_a, age_b, covar_a, covar_b)
Rhino Server contacting Site 1 and Site 2 for comorbidity counts…
✓  Comorbidity distribution ready — 6 bands received from Site 1 and Site 2    
Rhino Server contacting Site 1 and Site 2 for years since diagnosis…
✓  Years since diagnosis ready — 7 brackets received from Site 1 and Site 2

Which clinical signals move together?

Next we measure how strongly pairs of clinical variables move together at each hospital, using a correlation score (how strongly two measurements track each other, on a -1 to +1 scale) across several variables at once. Each hospital returns its own grid below — a correlation heatmap. Teal cells mark variables that rise together; amber cells mark variables that move in opposite directions; the darker the cell, the weaker the relationship. Seeing both sites side by side is far more revealing than studying either one in isolation: a pattern that is faint or absent in one cohort can stand out clearly in the other.

Why this matters
The two cohorts are genuinely different. Across 3,588 patients, the US network sees responses in 33.0% of patients, the EU registry in just 12.3% — about 20.4% combined. A model trained at one site alone would be blinkered by its own population. Combining both — without either one sharing a record — is exactly how it learns the fuller picture.
corr_a = build_corr_matrix(session, str(hospital_1_data.uid), CORR_VARS, "US Hospital")
corr_b = build_corr_matrix(session, str(hospital_2_data.uid), CORR_VARS, "EU Hospital")
plot_correlation_heatmaps(corr_a, corr_b, CORR_LABELS)
✓  Correlation matrix ready for US Hospital — 28 variable pairs                         
✓  Correlation matrix ready for EU Hospital — 28 variable pairs
3
Phase 3 of 4
Train a shared model — and prove it helps

Step 1: Agree on what "normal" looks like

Before any model can learn, both hospitals need to clean their data the same way. A single mistyped lab value can quietly skew a model, so we set aside records that fall far outside the normal range for each measurement.

Here is the catch: neither hospital can see the other's records, so neither one alone knows what "normal" looks like across the whole patient population. So we compute it jointly. Each site reports only the average and the spread of each measurement; from those summary numbers we agree on a shared normal range — any value more than three standard deviations from the combined average (a standard, conservative cutoff) is flagged for removal.

That shared definition is then shipped back to each hospital as an Auto Container (a small, sandboxed program sent to each site to run there), which does the cleaning locally. Same standard everywhere — so the model comparison later is a fair one.

The guarantee: Only the averages and spreads travel — never a single patient row. The raw records stay inside each hospital's walls.

TEST_FRACTION = 0.2

bounds = compute_outlier_bounds(session, all_uids, NUMERIC_FEATURES, zscore_threshold=3.0)
display_outlier_bounds(bounds)
✓  Outlier bounds ready — 7 features computed jointly across both hospitals
Federated 3σ Outlier Bounds — aggregated across both hospitals
  Lower Bound (μ − 3σ) Upper Bound (μ + 3σ)
Clinical Feature    
patient_age 10.95 45.67
baseline_tumor_marker -73,869.83 216,446.65
years_since_diagnosis -11.78 21.81
tumor_size_mm -10,555.88 32,680.15
pdl1_expression_pct 0.90 21.49
ctdna_vaf -0.34 0.78
months_on_prior_therapy -16.65 39.31

Step 2: Set aside a fair test

With the shared cleaning rule in hand, each hospital drops the records that fall outside the normal range, then splits the rest into two groups: one to train on, and one held back to test on later.

We hold back 20% of patients as an unseen test set, keeping the same share of treatment responders in each half so the test is honest — the model is graded on patients it never saw during training. This is the same care you would take in any local machine-learning workflow, applied across both hospitals at once.

Note: Both steps — the cleaning and the split — run inside a single program at each hospital, so only one execution is needed per site.

data_prep_code = build_data_prep_snippet(bounds, TEST_FRACTION, column_rename=COLUMN_RENAME)
deploy_and_run_snippet(
    session,
    project,
    "Outlier Removal & Test/Train Split",
    data_prep_code,
    datasets=[hospital_1_data, hospital_2_data],
    output_templates=[
        [f"{SITE_US_DATASET} - Train", f"{SITE_US_DATASET} - Test"],
        [f"{SITE_EU_DATASET} - Train", f"{SITE_EU_DATASET} - Test"],
    ],
    requirements=["pandas", "scikit-learn"],
)

hospital_1_train, hospital_2_train, hospital_1_test, hospital_2_test = fetch_datasets(
    session,
    project,
    [
        f"{SITE_US_DATASET} - Train",
        f"{SITE_EU_DATASET} - Train",
        f"{SITE_US_DATASET} - Test",
        f"{SITE_EU_DATASET} - Test",
    ],
)
train_uids = [str(hospital_1_train.uid), str(hospital_2_train.uid)]
test_uids = [str(hospital_1_test.uid), str(hospital_2_test.uid)]
print("Train/test split complete ✓")
Rhino Server deploying 'Outlier Removal & Test/Train Split' to both hospitals…
  Running at Project Lead… (1/2)
  Running at Site 2… (2/2)
✓  Data preparation complete — cleaned datasets registered at both hospitals

Step 3: The bake-off

Now the real test. To prove that collaboration actually helps, we train three contenders under identical conditions and let the results speak.

The model itself is XGBoost (a widely used machine-learning method that builds many small decision rules and combines them). The collaboration is coordinated by NVIDIA FLARE (a widely used NVIDIA technology that lets many sites train one model together): each hospital trains on its own patients, then sends back only what it learned — the model's adjustments — never the patient records themselves. After several rounds of this back-and-forth, the shared model has absorbed treatment-response patterns from both cohorts, yet no record ever left either site.

Three models, one fair experiment — the audience gets to judge which wins:

Contender Trained on
Hospital 1 model US patients only
Hospital 2 model EU patients only
Shared model Both cohorts, trained together

The guarantee: Only model updates cross the network. No raw patient record ever leaves either hospital.

import json

train_matrix = {
    "Hospital 1 Model": [hospital_1_train.uid],
    "Hospital 2 Model": [hospital_2_train.uid],
    "Global Model": [hospital_1_train.uid, hospital_2_train.uid],
}

with open("xgboost_flare/app/config/config_fed_client.json") as f:
    client_config = json.load(f)
with open("xgboost_flare/app/config/config_fed_server.json") as f:
    server_config = json.load(f)

code_runs = run_federated_training(
    session, project, CODE_OBJECT_NAME, train_matrix, client_config, server_config
)
print("All training runs complete ✓")
Rhino Server dispatching 3 training run(s) across federated sites…
  Waiting for 'Hospital 1 Model' to complete… [1/3]
  Waiting for 'Hospital 2 Model' to complete… [2/3]
  Waiting for 'Global Model' to complete… [3/3]
✓  All 3 training run(s) complete — model weights aggregated by Rhino Server
All training runs complete ✓

Does collaboration actually produce a better model?

We have three contenders: the US-only model, the EU-only model, and the shared model that learned from both. Now we put them to the test. Each model scores a set of held-back patients it has never seen — and the scoring runs locally at each hospital, using the same standard, transparent statistics any data scientist would use (the kind built into widely used tools like scikit-learn).

The privacy refrain still holds. Even at evaluation time, no raw patient record ever leaves either hospital. Only the resulting scores are compared.

The first scoreboard, plotted just below, is the ROC curve — a picture of how well each model separates patients who respond to the therapy from those who don't.

The headline metric
ROC-AUC measures how well a model ranks true responders above non-responders, across every possible cutoff. 1.0 is perfect; 0.5 is a coin flip. On the chart below, a line that hugs the top-left corner is the better model.
test_matrix = {
    "Hospital 1 Test Set": [hospital_1_test.uid],
    "Hospital 2 Test Set": [hospital_2_test.uid],
}

inference_results = run_inference_matrix(session, code_runs, test_matrix)
  Rhino Server sending 'Hospital 1 Model' to Hospital 1 Test Set for inference… [1/4]
  Rhino Server sending 'Hospital 2 Model' to Hospital 2 Test Set for inference… [2/4]
  Rhino Server sending 'Global Model' to Hospital 1 Test Set for inference… [3/4]
  Rhino Server sending 'Global Model' to Hospital 2 Test Set for inference… [4/4]
✓  All 4 inference run(s) complete — predictions generated locally at each hospital

metrics = compute_evaluation_metrics(session, inference_results)

plot_roc_curves(metrics)
Rhino Server computing evaluation metrics at each hospital…
  Evaluating 'Hospital 1 Model' across both hospitals… [1/3]
  Evaluating 'Hospital 2 Model' across both hospitals… [2/3]
  Evaluating 'Global Model' across both hospitals… [3/3]
✓  Evaluation metrics ready — 3 model(s) scored, no patient data left either site

One model, one yes-or-no call

The ROC curve grades a model across every possible cutoff. But in real life a clinician needs a single answer: is this patient likely to respond — yes or no? So we pick one cutoff — the decision threshold (the line above which a probability becomes a "yes") — set here at 0.5, and re-score all three models there.

At that fixed cutoff, four measures tell us whether the call is a good one:

Measure In plain terms
F1 Balances two mistakes equally: flagging a non-responder, and missing a real responder.
F2 The same idea, but it punishes missing a real responder more — the costlier error in cancer care.
Balanced Accuracy Average of "how many responders we caught" and "how many non-responders we correctly cleared" — fair even when responders are rare.
MCC A single overall grade for the yes/no calls, from -1 (always wrong) to +1 (always right).

The chart below shows these four alongside one ranking score, Average Precision (how well the model concentrates true responders at the top of its list) — five bars per model, the shared model against each go-it-alone model.

Why show five measures, not one? A model that wins on every measure is far more trustworthy than one tuned to look good on a single number. Showing all of them is how you prove the result isn't cherry-picked.

plot_scalar_metrics_and_confusion(metrics)
4
Phase 4 of 4
From experiment to bedside

Deploy the winning model to each hospital

A study is nice. Can we actually use this? Now we take the shared model that won the bake-off and put it to work — scoring new patients, one hospital at a time. We bundle the model into a self-contained, portable program so it can run anywhere, then send that program to each hospital. The patients get scored where they already are; nothing about them travels.

Step What happens
1 · Bring in patients Take in a new patient list — no treatment-outcome column needed; that is exactly what we are predicting.
2 · Score, in place Run the shared model locally inside each hospital. No patient record leaves the institution.
3 · Return answers Hand back two columns per patient: a scores column (a 0–1 likelihood of responding) and a predicted_class column (a plain yes/no call at the 0.5 mark).

The guarantee holds at the finish line, too. The model travels to each hospital; the patients never travel to the model. Only the predictions come back — no individual record, lab value, or treatment outcome ever leaves the institution that holds it.

From here it becomes routine clinical software. A better model can be rolled out as a new version, and its use can be tracked across both hospitals from one place — so the people relying on it always know which model is running and how it is performing.

For technical teams: a build-and-publish helper packages the model into Rhino's managed container registry for deployment.
inference_container = create_inference_container(
    session, project, code_runs["Global Model"], container_path="inference_container/"
)

inference_result = run_production_inference(
    session, inference_container, test_datasets=[hospital_1_test, hospital_2_test]
)

The takeaway

Two institutions collaborated to help patients without ever surrendering control of their data — and shipped the result. A US oncology network and an EU clinical registry, which cannot legally pool a single patient record, together built a model that predicts who will respond to a targeted therapy — then put it to work inside each hospital's own walls.

The payoff
The shared model out-scored both single-hospital models at both hospitals — 0.871 vs 0.802 in the US and 0.884 vs 0.819 in the EU on how well it ranks responders above non-responders (1.0 = perfect, 0.5 = a coin flip) — without a single patient record ever leaving either site.

That is the whole point: combining the two cohorts produced a better answer than either hospital could reach alone, and the privacy promise never broke. Here is how the four phases delivered it.

Phase What we used What it delivered
1 · Connect One governed workspace with built-in connectors to each hospital's existing databases Collaboration across US and EU hospitals with no data migration and no new infrastructure
2 · Explore Plain-language data queries and tooling that lines the two datasets up Understood both cohorts — and confirmed they differ enough that combining them should help — without moving any data
3 · Train & prove A sandboxed program shipped to each site, plus NVIDIA FLARE (the engine that lets many sites train one model together) Cleaned data, ran a fair three-model bake-off, and proved the shared model wins — raw records never leaving either hospital
4 · Deploy The winning model packaged as a portable program, with version control and usage monitoring Scored new patients locally at each hospital, with new versions easy to roll out and watch over time

This same pattern works for any multi-institution question where the data can't move — across hospitals, registries, biobanks, or borders. Bring the model to the data, and the answers come to you.
